analogy: Think of "Ecosystem" through the chapter's own opening images: An ecosystem can be visualised as a functional unit of nature, where living organisms interact among themselves and also with the surrounding physical environment. Ecosystem varies greatly in size from a small pond to a large forest or a sea. Many ecologists regard the entire... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Ecosystem is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that an ecosystem can be visualised as a functional unit of nature, where living organisms interact among themselves and also with the surrounding physical environment.

The text explains that ecosystem varies greatly in size from a small pond to a large forest or a sea.

The text explains that many ecologists regard the entire biosphere as a global ecosystem, as a composite of all local ecosystems on Earth.

The text explains that since this system is too much big and complex to be studied at one time, it is convenient to divide it into two basic categories, namely terrestrial and aquatic.

The text explains that forest, grassland and desert are some examples of terrestrial ecosystems; pond, lake, wetland, river and estuary are some examples of aquatic ecosystems.

The text explains that crop fields and an aquarium may also be considered as man-made ecosystems.

The text explains that we will first look at the structure of the ecosystem, in order to appreciate the input (productivity), transfer of energy (food chain/web, nutrient cycling) and the output (degradation and energy loss).

The text explains that we will also look at the relationships – cycles, chains, webs – that are created as a result of these energy flows within the system and their inter- relationship.

The text explains that cHAPTER 12 ECOSYSTEM 12.1 Ecosystem–Structure and Function 12.2.

The text explains that productivity 12.3 Decomposition 12.4 Energy Flow 12.5 Ecological Pyramids

The text explains that 206 BIOLOGY 12.1 ECOSYSTEM – STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION In earlier classes, you have looked at the various components of the environment- abiotic and biotic.

The text explains that you studied how the individual biotic and abiotic factors affected each other and their surrounding.

The text explains that let us look at these components in a more integrated manner and see how the flow of energy takes place within these components of the ecosystem.

The text explains that interaction of biotic and abiotic components result in a physical structure that is characteristic for each type of ecosystem.

The text explains that identification and enumeration of plant and animal species of an ecosystem gives its species composition.

The text explains that vertical distribution of different species occupying different levels is called stratification.

The text explains that the solar input, the cycle of temperature, day-length and other climatic conditions regulate the rate of function of the entire pond.

The text explains that this system performs all the functions of any ecosystem and of the biosphere as a whole, i.e., conversion of inorganic into organic material with the help of the radiant energy of the sun by the autotrophs; consumption of the autotrophs by heterotrophs; decomposition and mineralisation of the dead matter to release them back for reuse by the autotrophs, these event are repeated over and over again.

The text explains that there is unidirectional movement of energy towards the higher trophic levels and its dissipation and loss as heat to the environment.

The text explains that the important steps in the process of decomposition are fragmentation, leaching, catabolism, humification and mineralisation.

The text explains that humification and mineralisation occur during decomposition in the soil.

The text explains that the humus is further degraded by some microbes and release of inorganic nutrients occur by the process known as mineralisation.

The text explains that temperature and soil moisture are the most important climatic factors that regulate decomposition through their effects on the activities of soil microbes.

The text explains that warm and moist environment favour decomposition whereas low temperature and anaerobiosis inhibit decomposition resulting in build up of organic materials.

The text explains that 209 ECOSYSTEM 12.4 ENERGY FLOW Except for the deep sea hydro-thermal ecosystem, sun is the only source of energy for all ecosystems on Earth.

The text explains that all organisms are dependent for their food on producers, either directly or indirectly.

The text explains that so you find unidirectional flow of energy from the sun to producers and then to consumers.

The text explains that all animals depend on plants (directly or indirectly) for their food needs.

The text explains that based on the source of their nutrition or food, organisms occupy a specific place in the food chain that is known as their trophic level.

The text explains that when any organism dies it is converted to detritus or dead biomass that serves as an energy source for decomposers.

The text explains that based on source of nutrition every organism occupies a place in an ecosystem.

The text explains that atmosphere or hydrosphere is the reservoir for the gaseous type of cycle (carbon), whereas Earth’s crust is the reservoir for sedimentary type (phosphorus).

The text explains that which one of the following has the largest population in a food chain?

The text explains that (a) Producers (b) Primary consumers (c) Secondary consumers (d) Decomposers 3.

The text explains that the second trophic level in a lake is (a) Phytoplankton (b) Zooplankton (c) Benthos (d) Fishes 4.

The text explains that secondary producers are (a) Herbivores (b) Producers (c) Carnivores (d) None of the above 5.

The text explains that what is the percentage of photosynthetically active radiation (PAR) in the incident solar radiation?

The text explains that distinguish between (a) Grazing food chain and detritus food chain (b) Production and decomposition (c) Upright and inverted pyramid (d) Food chain and Food web (e) Litter and detritus (f) Primary and secondary productivity 7.

The text explains that define ecological pyramids and describe with examples, pyramids of number and biomass.

The text explains that give brief description of factors that affect primary productivity.

The text explains that define decomposition and describe the processes and products of decomposition.

The text explains that give an account of energy flow in an ecosystem.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12: Ecosystem

prelims_traps: Do not treat CHAPTER 12 and ECOSYSTEM as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Ecosystem; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Ecosystem through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use CHAPTER 12 and ECOSYSTEM to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
